# Tab Group

                          `<sl-tab-group> | SlTabGroup`                                       Since 2.0                stable                                 

            Tab groups organize content into a container that shows one section at a time.                     

            Tab groups make use of tabs and             tab panels. Each tab must be slotted into the `nav` slot and             its `panel` must refer to a tab panel of the same name.                                                               General                Custom                Advanced                Disabled                This is the general tab panel.                This is the custom tab panel.                This is the advanced tab panel.                This is a disabled tab panel.                                                                                                                

```
<sl-tab-group>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="general">General</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="custom">Custom</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="advanced">Advanced</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="disabled" disabled>Disabled</sl-tab>

  <sl-tab-panel name="general">This is the general tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="custom">This is the custom tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="advanced">This is the advanced tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="disabled">This is a disabled tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
</sl-tab-group>

```

###             Closable Tabs          

          

            Add the `closable` attribute to a tab to show a close button. This example shows how you can             dynamically remove tabs from the DOM when the close button is activated.                                                               General                Closable 1                Closable 2                Closable 3                This is the general tab panel.                This is the first closable tab panel.                This is the second closable tab panel.                This is the third closable tab panel.                                                                                                                              

```
<sl-tab-group class="tabs-closable">
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="general">General</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="closable-1" closable>Closable 1</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="closable-2" closable>Closable 2</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="closable-3" closable>Closable 3</sl-tab>

  <sl-tab-panel name="general">This is the general tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="closable-1">This is the first closable tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="closable-2">This is the second closable tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="closable-3">This is the third closable tab panel.</sl-tab-panel>
</sl-tab-group>

<script>
  const tabGroup = document.querySelector('.tabs-closable');

  tabGroup.addEventListener('sl-close', async event => {
    const tab = event.target;
    const panel = tabGroup.querySelector(`sl-tab-panel[name="${tab.panel}"]`);

    // Show the previous tab if the tab is currently active
    if (tab.active) {
      tabGroup.show(tab.previousElementSibling.panel);
    }

    // Remove the tab + panel
    tab.remove();
    panel.remove();
  });
</script>

```
                                            

```
import SlTab from '@shoelace-style/shoelace/dist/react/tab';
import SlTabGroup from '@shoelace-style/shoelace/dist/react/tab-group';
import SlTabPanel from '@shoelace-style/shoelace/dist/react/tab-panel';

const App = () => {
  function handleClose(event) {
    //
    // This is a crude example that removes the tab and its panel from the DOM.
    // There are better ways to manage tab creation/removal in React, but that
    // would significantly complicate the example.
    //
    const tab = event.target;
    const tabGroup = tab.closest('sl-tab-group');
    const tabPanel = tabGroup.querySelector(`[aria-labelledby="${tab.id}"]`);

    tab.remove();
    tabPanel.remove();
  }

  return (
    <SlTabGroup className="tabs-closable" onSlClose={handleClose}>
      <SlTab slot="nav" panel="general">
        General
      </SlTab>
      <SlTab slot="nav" panel="closable-1" closable onSlClose={handleClose}>
        Closable 1
      </SlTab>
      <SlTab slot="nav" panel="closable-2" closable onSlClose={handleClose}>
        Closable 2
      </SlTab>
      <SlTab slot="nav" panel="closable-3" closable onSlClose={handleClose}>
        Closable 3
      </SlTab>

      <SlTabPanel name="general">This is the general tab panel.</SlTabPanel>
      <SlTabPanel name="closable-1">This is the first closable tab panel.</SlTabPanel>
      <SlTabPanel name="closable-2">This is the second closable tab panel.</SlTabPanel>
      <SlTabPanel name="closable-3">This is the third closable tab panel.</SlTabPanel>
    </SlTabGroup>
  );
};

```

Based on the documentation provided, here are the key points about using Shoelace tab groups:

1. Basic Structure:
```html
<sl-tab-group>
  <!-- Tabs -->
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="panel1">Tab 1</sl-tab>
  <sl-tab slot="nav" panel="panel2">Tab 2</sl-tab>

  <!-- Panels -->
  <sl-tab-panel name="panel1">Content 1</sl-tab-panel>
  <sl-tab-panel name="panel2">Content 2</sl-tab-panel>
</sl-tab-group>
```

2. Key Features:
- Tab placement options: top (default), bottom, start, end
- Closable tabs with the `closable` attribute
- Scrollable tabs when content overflows
- Manual or automatic activation modes
- Disabled tabs support

3. Main Properties:
- `placement`: Controls tab position ('top', 'bottom', 'start', 'end')
- `activation`: Controls tab activation behavior ('auto' or 'manual')
- `no-scroll-controls`: Disables scroll arrows
- `fixed-scroll-controls`: Keeps scroll controls visible

4. Events:
- `sl-tab-show`: Fired when a tab is shown
- `sl-tab-hide`: Fired when a tab is hidden

5. Methods:
- `show(panel)`: Programmatically show a specific tab panel

6. Customization:
- CSS custom properties for styling:
  - `--indicator-color`: Active tab indicator color
  - `--track-color`: Track line color
  - `--track-width`: Track line width

7. React Usage Example:
```jsx
import SlTabGroup from '@shoelace-style/shoelace/dist/react/tab-group';
import SlTab from '@shoelace-style/shoelace/dist/react/tab';
import SlTabPanel from '@shoelace-style/shoelace/dist/react/tab-panel';

const App = () => (
  <SlTabGroup>
    <SlTab slot="nav" panel="tab1">Tab 1</SlTab>
    <SlTabPanel name="tab1">Content 1</SlTabPanel>
  </SlTabGroup>
);
```

```typescript
export class RouteMap {
  private map: L.Map | null = null;
  private mapData: MapData | null = null;

  constructor() {
    // Find all tab panels with maps
    document.querySelectorAll('[data-tab-panel="map"]').forEach(element => {
      const tabPanel = element.closest('sl-tab-panel');
      if (tabPanel) {
        // Watch for the Shoelace tab panel show event
        tabPanel.addEventListener('sl-show', () => {
          this.initMap(element as HTMLElement);
        });
      }
    });
  }

  private initMap(element: HTMLElement) {
    if (this.map) return; // Only initialize once

    // Try to get the map data
    const mapDataElement = document.getElementById('route-map-data');
    if (!mapDataElement) {
      console.error('No map data found');
      return;
    }

    try {
      this.mapData = JSON.parse(mapDataElement.textContent || '');
    } catch (error) {
      console.error('Failed to parse map data:', error);
      return;
    }

    // Initialize the map
    this.map = L.map(element);

    // Add OpenStreetMap tiles
    L.tileLayer('https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png', {
      attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors'
    }).addTo(this.map);

    this.addMarkers();
    this.addRoute();
    this.fitBounds();
  }

  private addMarkers() {
    if (!this.map || !this.mapData) return;

    this.mapData.waypoints.forEach((point, index) => {
      const icon = L.divIcon({
        html: `
          <div class="_w-6 _h-6 _rounded-full _bg-white _border-2 _border-primary _flex _items-center _justify-center">
            ${index + 1}
          </div>
        `,
        className: 'custom-div-icon'
      });

      L.marker(point.coords, { icon })
        .bindPopup(point.name)
        .addTo(this.map!);
    });
  }

  private addRoute() {
    if (!this.map || !this.mapData?.routeData.coordinates) return;

    L.polyline(this.mapData.routeData.coordinates, {
      color: 'var(--sl-color-primary-600)',
      weight: 4
    }).addTo(this.map);
  }

  private fitBounds() {
    if (!this.map || !this.mapData?.routeData.bbox) return;

    const bbox = this.mapData.routeData.bbox;
    this.map.fitBounds([
      [bbox[1], bbox[0]], // southwest
      [bbox[3], bbox[2]]  // northeast
    ]);
  }
}
```

To maintain the specific order while being efficient with queries, you can use the `__in` lookup with `Case` expressions to order the results exactly as needed. Here's how to modify the query:

```python
from django.db.models import Case, When, IntegerField

# ... rest of the code ...

    # Create ordered mapping of IDs

    # Single query that gets addresses in correct order
    addresses = list(
        Address.objects.filter(id__in=address_order).annotate(
            custom_order=Case(
                *[When(id=id, then=pos) for pos, id in enumerate(address_order)],
                output_field=IntegerField(),
            )
        ).order_by('custom_order')
    )

    # Now addresses will be in the exact order needed for fetch_route()
    route_response = fetch_route(addresses)
```

```ts cloud/cloud/fieldsales/static/travel-map.ts
  coords: [number, number]; // [lat, lng]

interface RouteData {
  coordinates: [number, number][]; // array of [lat, lng]
  bbox: [number, number, number, number]; // [west, south, east, north]
}

interface MapData {
```

```typescript
  routeData: RouteData;
}

// Keep track of initialized maps
const maps = new WeakMap<HTMLElement, L.Map>();

document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
  document.addEventListener('sl-tab-show', (event: Event) => {
    const tab = event as CustomEvent;
    if (tab.detail.name !== 'travel-map') {
      console.log('Ignoring tab show event - not travel-map tab:', tab.detail.name);
      return;
    }

    const tabPanel = tab.detail.panel as HTMLElement;
    if (!tabPanel) {
      console.log('No tab panel found in event details:', tab.detail);
      return;
    }

    // Check if map is already initialized for this panel
    if (maps.has(tabPanel)) {
      console.log('Map already initialized for this tab panel');
      return;
    }

    // Find map container and data within this specific tab panel
    const mapElement = tabPanel.querySelector('[data-travel-map]');
    if (!(mapElement instanceof HTMLElement)) {
      console.error('Map element not found in tab panel. Contents:', tabPanel.innerHTML);
      return;
    }

    const mapDataElement = tabPanel.querySelector('[data-travel-map-data]');
    if (!mapDataElement?.textContent) {
      console.error('Map data not found in tab panel or is empty. Contents:', tabPanel.innerHTML);
      mapElement.innerHTML = '<div class="_p-4">Error loading map</div>';
      return;
    }

    try {
      const { waypoints, routeData }: MapData = JSON.parse(mapDataElement.textContent);
      console.log('Parsed map data:', { waypoints, routeData });

      // Initialize map
      const map = L.map(mapElement);
      maps.set(tabPanel, map);
      console.log('Map initialized');

      // Add OpenStreetMap tiles
      L.tileLayer('https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png', {
        attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors',
      }).addTo(map);

      // Add markers for waypoints
      waypoints.forEach((point, index) => {
        const icon = L.divIcon({
          html: `
            <div class="_w-6 _h-6 _rounded-full _bg-white _border-2 _border-primary _flex _items-center _justify-center">
              ${index + 1}
            </div>
          `,
          className: 'custom-div-icon',
        });

        L.marker(point.coords, { icon })
          .bindPopup(point.name)
          .addTo(map);
      });
      console.log('Added', waypoints.length, 'markers to map');

      // Add route line
      if (routeData.coordinates) {
        L.polyline(routeData.coordinates, {
          color: 'var(--sl-color-primary-600)',
          weight: 4,
        }).addTo(map);
        console.log('Added route line to map');

        // Fit map to route bounds
        if (routeData.bbox) {
          console.log('Fitting map to bounds:', routeData.bbox);
          setTimeout(() => {
            map.invalidateSize(true);
            map.fitBounds([
              [routeData.bbox[1], routeData.bbox[0]], // southwest
              [routeData.bbox[3], routeData.bbox[2]], // northeast
            ]);
            console.log('Map bounds updated');
          }, 100);
        } else {
          console.log('No bbox available for route');
        }
      } else {
        console.log('No coordinates available for route');
      }
    } catch (error) {
      console.error('Failed to initialize map:', error);
      console.error('Raw map data content:', mapDataElement.textContent);
      mapElement.innerHTML = '<div class="_p-4">Error loading map</div>';
    }
  });
  console.log('Tab show event listener initialized');
});
```

```py cloud/cloud/travel/distance_api.py
from __future__ import annotations

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from functools import partial
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

from django.contrib.gis.geos import LineString

import requests
from rest_framework import status
from urllib3 import Retry

from cloud import metrics
from cloud.travel.enums import Source
from cloud.travel.interface import DistanceResponse, RouteResponse

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from cloud.location.models import Address


distance_api_session = requests.Session()
adapter = requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter(max_retries=Retry(read=3, connect=5, redirect=10, total=10))
distance_api_session.mount('http://', adapter)
distance_api_session.mount('https://', adapter)


def fetch_single(from_address: Address, to_address: Address) -> DistanceResponse | None:
    with metrics.requests('distance-api', 'route', session=distance_api_session) as session:
        assert from_address.point is not None
        assert to_address.point is not None
        from_address_longitude, from_address_latitude = from_address.point
        to_address_longitude, to_address_latitude = to_address.point
        with session.get(
            'https://distance-api-v2.otovo.com/route',
            params=dict(
                point=[
                    f'{from_address_latitude},{from_address_longitude}',
                    f'{to_address_latitude},{to_address_longitude}',
                ],
                profile='car',
                instructions='false',
                avoid='ferry',
                calc_points='false',
                points_encoded='false',
            ),
        ) as response:
            if response.status_code != status.HTTP_200_OK:
                return None

            if (data := response.json()) and (paths := data.get('paths', [])):
                return DistanceResponse(
                    source=Source.DISTANCE_API,
                    from_address=from_address,
                    to_address=to_address,
                    meters=paths[0]['distance'],
                    minutes=(paths[0]['time'] / 1000) // 60,
                )


def fetch_route(addresses: list[Address]) -> RouteResponse | None:
    points = [address.point for address in addresses]
    assert all(points)

    with metrics.requests('distance-api', 'route', session=distance_api_session) as session:
        with session.get(
            'https://distance-api-v2.otovo.com/route',
            params=dict(
                point=[f'{point.y},{point.x}' for point in points],
                profile='car',
                instructions='false',
                avoid='ferry',
                calc_points='true',
                points_encoded='false',
            ),
        ) as response:
            if response.status_code != status.HTTP_200_OK:
                return None
            if (data := response.json()) and (paths := data.get('paths', [])):
                return RouteResponse(
                    source=Source.DISTANCE_API,
                    addresses=addresses,
                    meters=paths[0]['distance'],
                    minutes=(paths[0]['time'] / 1000) // 60,
                    route=LineString(paths[0]['points']['coordinates']),
                    bbox=paths[0]['bbox'],
                )


class DistanceAPI:
    @classmethod
    def get_distance(cls, from_address: Address, to_address: Address) -> DistanceResponse | None:
        result = cls.get_distances([from_address], to_address)
        return result[0] if result else None

    @classmethod
    def get_distances(cls, from_addresses: list[Address], to_address: Address) -> list[DistanceResponse]:
        results = []
        if len(from_addresses) > 3:
            with ThreadPoolExecutor() as pool:
                return [
                    result
                    for result in pool.map(partial(fetch_single, to_address=to_address), from_addresses)
                    if result
                ]
        for from_address in from_addresses:
            if entry := fetch_single(from_address, to_address):
                results.append(entry)
        return results
```

Explain why Sequence is better than List
`Sequence` is better than `List` in this case due to variance rules in Python's type system. Here's the explanation:

1. **Variance in Type Systems**:
- **Invariant** (List): Cannot substitute a more specific or more general type
- **Covariant** (Sequence): Can substitute with a more specific type
- **Contravariant**: Can substitute with a more general type

2. **List is Invariant**:
```python
class Animal: pass
class Dog(Animal): pass

# This won't work with List
def process_animals(animals: List[Animal]): pass
dogs: List[Dog] = [Dog()]
process_animals(dogs)  # Type error! List[Dog] cannot be used where List[Animal] is expected
```

3. **Sequence is Covariant**:
```python
from typing import Sequence

def process_animals(animals: Sequence[Animal]): pass
dogs: List[Dog] = [Dog()]
process_animals(dogs)  # Works! Because List[Dog] can be treated as Sequence[Animal]
```

4. **Why List is Invariant**:
```python
def modify_animals(animals: List[Animal]):
    animals.append(Cat())  # If List[Dog] was allowed, this would break type safety!

dogs: List[Dog] = [Dog()]
modify_animals(dogs)  # This should not be allowed
```

5. **Why Sequence Works**:
- It's read-only (no append, extend, etc.)
- Cannot accidentally put wrong types into the collection
- Still provides iteration, length, indexing
- More flexible in accepting different collection types

In your case:
```python
# Original (problematic for mypy):
def fetch_route(addresses: list[Address]) -> RouteResponse | None: ...

# The annotated Address list from your query could be a subtype of Address
addresses = Address.objects.annotate(...).order_by(...)  # Has extra attributes

# Better with Sequence:
def fetch_route(addresses: Sequence[Address]) -> RouteResponse | None: ...
```

The main benefits are:
1. More flexible - accepts any sequence-like object
2. Better type safety - prevents modification
3. Clearer intent - shows you only need read access
4. Works with Django querysets and annotated models

Real world example from your code:
```python
# Your annotated Address has extra fields (travel_order)
addresses = Address.objects.annotate(
    travel_order=Case(...)
).order_by('travel_order')

# With List:
fetch_route(addresses)  # Type error! List[AnnotatedAddress] ≠ List[Address]

# With Sequence:
fetch_route(addresses)  # Works! AnnotatedAddress is still an Address
```

This is why mypy suggested using `Sequence` - it's more appropriate for your use case where you're passing potentially annotated model instances to a function that only needs to read them.
how do I configure leaflet with fallbacks in case a tile server is down?
Here's how to configure Leaflet with multiple tile servers as fallbacks:

```typescript
// Initialize map with fallback tile layers
const tileLayerConfigs = [
  {
    url: 'https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png',
    attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors',
  },
  {
    url: 'https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{z}/{x}/{y}.png',
    attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles style by Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team',
  },
  {
    url: 'https://tiles.stadiamaps.com/tiles/osm_bright/{z}/{x}/{y}{r}.png',
    attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles by Stadia Maps',
  }
];

// Create a layer group for redundancy
const redundantTileLayers = tileLayerConfigs.map(config => 
  L.tileLayer(config.url, {
    attribution: config.attribution,
    maxZoom: 19,
    // Add error handling
    errorTileUrl: 'path/to/fallback-tile.png', // Optional fallback tile image
  })
);

// Add all layers to the map with only the first one visible
const layerGroup = L.layerGroup(redundantTileLayers).addTo(map);
let currentLayerIndex = 0;

// Function to handle tile loading errors
const handleTileError = () => {
  console.log(`Tile layer ${currentLayerIndex} failed, trying next...`);
  
  // Hide current layer and show next
  redundantTileLayers[currentLayerIndex].removeFrom(map);
  currentLayerIndex = (currentLayerIndex + 1) % redundantTileLayers.length;
  redundantTileLayers[currentLayerIndex].addTo(map);
};

// Add error handlers to all layers
redundantTileLayers.forEach(layer => {
  layer.on('tileerror', handleTileError);
});

// Start with first layer
redundantTileLayers[0].addTo(map);
```

```typescript
  },
  {
    url: 'https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{z}/{x}/{y}.png',
    attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles by HOT',
    subdomains: ['a', 'b', 'c'],
    maxRetries: 3,
    timeout: 5000,
  },
  // Add more providers as needed
];

class ResilientTileLayer {
  private layers: L.TileLayer[] = [];
  private currentIndex = 0;
  private retryCount = 0;
  private map: L.Map;

  constructor(map: L.Map, configs: TileLayerConfig[]) {
    this.map = map;
    this.layers = configs.map(config => this.createLayer(config));
    this.addCurrentLayer();
  }

  private createLayer(config: TileLayerConfig): L.TileLayer {
    const layer = L.tileLayer(config.url, {
      attribution: config.attribution,
      maxZoom: 19,
      subdomains: config.subdomains || ['a', 'b', 'c'],
      errorTileUrl: 'path/to/fallback-tile.png',
    });

    layer.on('tileerror', () => this.handleTileError(config));
    return layer;
  }

  private handleTileError(config: TileLayerConfig) {
    if (this.retryCount < (config.maxRetries || 3)) {
      this.retryCount++;
      console.log(`Retry ${this.retryCount} for layer ${this.currentIndex}`);
      
      // Retry current layer after timeout
      setTimeout(() => {
        this.layers[this.currentIndex].redraw();
      }, 1000 * this.retryCount); // Exponential backoff
      
    } else {
      this.retryCount = 0;
      this.switchToNextLayer();
    }
  }

  private switchToNextLayer() {
    console.log(`Switching from layer ${this.currentIndex} to next layer`);
    
    // Remove current layer
    this.layers[this.currentIndex].removeFrom(this.map);
    
    // Switch to next layer
    this.currentIndex = (this.currentIndex + 1) % this.layers.length;
    this.addCurrentLayer();
  }

  private addCurrentLayer() {
    const layer = this.layers[this.currentIndex];
    layer.addTo(this.map);

    // Add timeout for initial load
    const config = tileLayerConfigs[this.currentIndex];
    if (config.timeout) {
      setTimeout(() => {
        if (!layer.isLoading()) return;
        console.log(`Layer ${this.currentIndex} timed out`);
        this.switchToNextLayer();
      }, config.timeout);
    }
  }
}

// Usage in your map initialization:
try {
  const map = L.map(mapElement);
  new ResilientTileLayer(map, tileLayerConfigs);
  
  // Rest of your map initialization...
} catch (error) {
  console.error('Failed to initialize map:', error);
  mapElement.innerHTML = '<div class="_p-4">Error loading map</div>';
}
```

This implementation:
1. Supports multiple tile servers
2. Includes retry logic with exponential backoff
3. Has timeouts for initial loading
4. Handles subdomain configuration
5. Provides fallback tile images
6. Logs failures for debugging

```typescript
}

const metrics: MetricEvent[] = [];

private logMetric(event: Omit<MetricEvent, 'timestamp'>) {
  metrics.push({
    ...event,

  // Could send to your metrics service

}
```

And use it in your error handling:

```typescript
  this.logMetric({
    provider: config.url,

}
```

so just adding two won't make leaflet automatically show the top one if it's working and the fallback if it's not? 
Actually, you're right - I overengineered it. For simple fallback behavior, you can just add multiple tile layers and Leaflet will automatically try the next one if the first fails. Here's the simpler approach:

```typescript
// Initialize map with primary and fallback tile layers
L.tileLayer('https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png', {
  attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors'
}).addTo(map);

// Add fallback
L.tileLayer('https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{z}/{x}/{y}.png', {
  attribution: '© OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles by HOT'
}).addTo(map);
```

Leaflet will automatically:
1. Try loading tiles from the first source
2. If that fails, it will try the second source
3. Both layers are managed independently

Yes! Given the three-point journey (previous -> destination -> next), we can create two differently colored polylines to show the "to destination" and "from destination" parts of the route. Here's how to modify the code:

```typescript
// Add route lines with different colors
if (routeData.coordinates) {
  // Find the middle point (destination) index
  const midPoint = Math.floor(routeData.coordinates.length / 2);
  
  // Split coordinates into two routes
  const toDestination = routeData.coordinates.slice(0, midPoint);
  const fromDestination = routeData.coordinates.slice(midPoint);

  // Add polyline for route to destination
  L.polyline(toDestination, {
    color: 'var(--sl-color-primary-600)', // or any color for "to destination"
    weight: 4,
  }).addTo(map);

  // Add polyline for route from destination
  L.polyline(fromDestination, {
    color: 'var(--sl-color-grey-400)', // or any color for "from destination"
    weight: 4,
    dashArray: '5, 10', // Optional: make this line dashed to further differentiate
  }).addTo(map);
}
```

This will:
1. Split the route at the destination point
2. Show the first part (previous -> destination) in one color
3. Show the second part (destination -> next) in another color

# Duotone Icons

        

If you’re looking to add multiple colors or tones or swap the layer opacity of your icons, you’ve come to the right place. Who doesn’t love a winning flavor combination?

Our duotone icons (in both the Duotone and Sharp Duotone styles) work just like all of our other icons but with two distinct shades of color. Duotone is a great effect when you’re looking to add more of your brand or an illustrative quality to the icons in your project. 

  Before You Get Started    

Make sure you’re:
- Set up with Font Awesome in your project.
- Familiar with the basics of adding Font Awesome icons.
- An active Pro-level plan subscriber or have a Pro license with access to Version 6.

    

  Double the Fun with Less Hassle   

All the styles in the Duotone and Sharp Duotone families work with the duotone styling properties described on this page. So once you get the hang of styling duotone icons, take all the duotone styles for a spin!  

## Basic Use

Duotone and Sharp Duotone icons use the same syntax Font Awesome icons, and you can reference them like any other icon. They will inherit whatever color is around them, with the secondary layer being a transparent version of the primary color.   

```
<div class="fa-3x">  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-camera"></i> <!-- a solid duotone camera icon -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-fire-alt"></i> <!-- a solid duotone fire-alt icon -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt"></i> <!-- a solid sharp-duotone bus-alt icon -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-fill-drip"></i> <!-- a solid sharp-duotone fill-drip icon --></div>
```

## Swapping Layer Opacity

You can swap the default opacity of each duotone icon’s layers. This will make an icon’s primary layer have the default opacity of 40% rather than its secondary layer.   

```
<div class="fa-3x">  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-camera"></i> <!-- a duotone solid camera icon -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-camera fa-swap-opacity"></i> <!-- a duotone solid camera icon with swapped opacity -->
  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-fire-alt"></i> <!-- a duotone solid fire-alt icon -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-fire-alt fa-swap-opacity"></i> <!-- a duotone solid fire-alt icon with swapped opacity -->
  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt"></i> <!-- a sharp duotone solid bus-alt icon -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt fa-swap-opacity"></i> <!-- a sharp duotone solid bus-alt icon with swapped opacity --></div>
```

Swap until you drop with these Codepen opacity-swapping examples.  

## Changing Opacity

By default, the secondary layer in a duotone icon is set to 40% opacity (via an `opacity 0.4;` rule in Font Awesome’s support CSS). You can **explicitly set the opacity of a duotone icon’s layer** by using the CSS custom properties below. New to custom properties? Here are some recommendations on using them in a project.

| Properties | CSS Custom Property | Accepted Values |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Set Primary Layer Opacity | `--fa-primary-opacity` | `0`  `1.0` |
| Set Secondary Layer Opacity | `--fa-secondary-opacity` | `0`  `1.0` |   

```
<div class="fa-3x">  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-secondary-opacity: 0.20"></i> <!--  secondary layer's opacity set to 20% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-secondary-opacity: 0.40"></i> <!--  secondary layer's opacity set to 40% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-secondary-opacity: 0.60"></i> <!--  secondary layer's opacity set to 60% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-secondary-opacity: 0.80"></i> <!--  secondary layer's opacity set to 80% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-secondary-opacity: 1.0"></i> <!--  secondary layer's opacity set to 100% --></div>
```
   

```
<div class="fa-3x">  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-opacity: 0.20"></i> <!--  primary layer's opacity set to 20% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-opacity: 0.40"></i> <!--  primary layer's opacity set to 40% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-opacity: 0.60"></i> <!--  primary layer's opacity set to 60% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-opacity: 0.80"></i> <!--  primary layer's opacity set to 80% -->  <i class="fa-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-opacity: 1.0"></i> <!--  primary layer's opacity set to 100% --></div>
```

## Coloring Duotone Icons

Like all other Font Awesome icons, duotone icons automatically inherit CSS size and color. A duotone icon consists of a **primary** and **secondary** layer. By default, The secondary layer is given an opacity of 40% so that it appears as a lighter shade of the icon’s inherited or directly set color.

Using CSS custom properties, we’ve also added some color hooks to a duotone icon’s primary and secondary layers. New to custom properties? Here are some places to set them.

| Properties | CSS Custom Property | Accepted Values |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Set Primary Layer Color | `--fa-primary-color` | Any valid CSS `color` value |
| Set Secondary Layer Color | `--fa-secondary-color` | Any valid CSS `color` value |   

```
<div class="fa-3x">  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-color: gold;"></i> <!-- primary layer color defined -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-bus-alt" style="--fa-primary-color: orangered;"></i> <!-- primary layer color defined -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-fill-drip" style="--fa-secondary-color: limegreen;"></i> <!-- secondary layer color defined -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-fill-drip" style="--fa-secondary-color: rebeccapurple;"></i> <!-- secondary layer color defined -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-battery-full" style="--fa-primary-color: limegreen; --fa-secondary-color: dimgray;"></i> <!-- secondary + primary layer color defined -->  <i class="fa-sharp-duotone fa-solid fa-battery-quarter" style="--fa-primary-color: orange; --fa-secondary-color: dimgray;"></i> <!-- secondary + primary layer color defined --></div>
```

## Using in a Project

CSS custom properties are still a pretty new thing for most folks. We’ve documented some of the ways you can define them within your project.

## Accessibility Best Practices

Because duotone icons are made up of two layers with different color values, they can be more like illustrations than other Font Awesome styles and need extra care for accessibility.

Since they are more complex visually, duotone icons may be harder to read at smaller sizes. In addition to that, the colors and opacity you choose for each icon’s layers can also affect their legibility. Some tips for improving legibility and accessibility:
- Render duotone style icons at larger sizes (2x your base type size is a good starting point).
- Test layer colors together to make sure they have enough color contrast.
- Test layer colors together to make sure they handle color-blindness scenarios well.

## Custom Icon Uploads Are Like Onions

They now have layers! You can add your very own duotone creations to a Kit if you have a Pro-level plan. Get up to speed on how best to make some, and how to use them once they’re in your Kit.

## Duotone Classes

| Class | Details |
| --- | --- |
| `fa-swap-opacity` | Swap the default opacity of each layer in a duotone icon (making an icon’s primary layer have the default opacity of 40% rather than its secondary layer) |

## Customization

We’ve added CSS custom properties to make customizing easier and more efficient. Define your own values for the following properties to override and customize Font Awesome’s defaults.

| CSS Custom Property | Details | Accepted Values |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `--fa-primary-color` | Set primary layer color | Any valid CSS `color` value |
| `--fa-primary-opacity` | Set primary layer opacity | `0`  `1.0` |
| `--fa-secondary-color` | Set secondary layer color | Any valid CSS `color` value |
| `--fa-secondary-opacity` | Set secondary layer opacity | `0`  `1.0` |

## Troubleshooting

### Can I use Duotone Icons by using CSS pseudo-elements?

Yes, duotone icons can be added to your web projects as CSS pseudo-elements. Doing so is even more complicated than the base difficulty of rendering icons with CSS pseudo-elements though. It’s recommended only for those who can’t control the final markup of their site/project as well as ninjas who want complete control over their markup.

### Doh! The icon I want to use isn’t showing up as a duotone icon!

Let’s double-check a few things.
- Are you using the duotone-specific `fa-duotone` or `fa-sharp-duotone` family prefix?
- Duotone icons are only available with Font Awesome Pro — make sure you have an active Pro-level plan or a Pro license with access to **Version 5.10.0 or greater** for regular Duotone or **Version 6.6.0 or greater** for Sharp Duotone icons.
- We release icons pretty frequently these days. Make sure you’re using the latest and greatest version, so any new icons you want to use are at your fingertips.
- If you’ve decided to host your own copy of Font Awesome, check that you’ve moved all of the files you need to use the icon you want. Also, double-check the paths to those files in your HTML’s `<head>`.

### Help! I can’t target individual layers!

Make sure you’re using the correct custom property values. Also, make sure you haven’t defined a custom property that may be overriding things (check inline and in your custom CSS). We don’t recommend writing custom CSS to target a specific class or pseudo-element — there are some differences between our Web Fonts and SVG versions of Font Awesome that can make this tricky.

### Can I use Font Awesome styling with duotone icons?

Yes! You can use any of our styling options - like sizing, fixed-width icons, and animations just to name a few - with duotone icons. And if you’re using the SVG + JS version of Font Awesome, our power transforms and layering let you do some pretty amazing stuff.

### I’m using a subsetted Kit and the layers of my duotone icon layers look weird!

If you are using a Kit where you have selected subsetting, it is possible for the layers of your duotone icon to be rendered separately. This is most likely caused by attempting to use a duotone style that is not included in the subset. Here’s the fix.
- Check the style of your icon and make sure the style you are trying to use is a part of your Kit subset.

### Do duotone icons work with all browsers?

Duotone icons render great in all modern web browsers. Since Internet Explorer versions 10 and 11 do not support CSS Custom properties, duotone icons will still render but you won’t be able to define colors or opacity for individual layers. If you must support that outdated browser, we recommend defining the color on the icon or its parent element instead.            Previous  Layering Text & Counters       Next  Customizing Icons

Here's two dataclassees we use for slots:

```py
@dataclass(unsafe_hash=True)
class SlotWithTravelTime:
    slot: DateTimeTZRange
    travel_time_to_destination: DateTimeTZRange  # Travel time from home/previous home visit
    travel_time_from_destination: DateTimeTZRange  # Travel time to the next home visit/starting address
    destination: Address
    previous_address: Address
    next_address: Address
    agent_id: uuid.UUID
    schedule_id: uuid.UUID
    unavailable: bool = False

    @property
    def start_time(self) -> datetime.datetime:
        return self.slot.lower or datetime.datetime.min

    @property
    def end_time(self) -> datetime.datetime:
        return self.slot.upper or datetime.datetime.max

    @property
    def travel_duration_to_destination(self) -> int:
        return int(
            (self.travel_time_to_destination.upper - self.travel_time_to_destination.lower).total_seconds()
        )

    @property
    def travel_duration_from_destination(self) -> int:
        return int(
            (
                self.travel_time_from_destination.upper - self.travel_time_from_destination.lower
            ).total_seconds()
        )

    @property
    def combined_travel_duration(self) -> int:
        return self.travel_duration_to_destination + self.travel_duration_from_destination

    def __eq__(self, value: object, /) -> bool:
        if not isinstance(value, SlotWithTravelTime):
            return False
        return self.slot == value.slot and self.agent_id == value.agent_id

    def get_time_until_appointment(self, from_time: datetime.datetime) -> int:
        return round((self.start_time - from_time).total_seconds())


@dataclass
class SlotWithScore(SlotWithTravelTime):
    score: float = 0
    rank: int = 0

    @property
    def is_best(self):
        return self.rank == 1

    @property
    def is_second_best(self):
        return 1 < self.rank < 4

    @property
    def detail_vals(self) -> dict[str, str]:
        return {
            'travel_to_slot_start_time': self.travel_time_to_destination.lower.isoformat(),
            # TODO: I think we should double check the timezone here
            'start_time': self.start_time.isoformat(),
            'end_time': self.end_time.isoformat(),
            'travel_from_slot_end_time': self.travel_time_from_destination.upper.isoformat(),
            'rank': str(self.rank),
            'score': str(self.score),
            'schedule': str(self.schedule_id),
            'destination_address_id': str(self.destination.pk),
            'previous_address_id': str(self.previous_address.pk),
            'next_address_id': str(self.next_address.pk),
        }

    @property
    def css_class(self):
        return f'{self.agent_id}{int(self.slot.lower.timestamp())}'
```

```py
@require_http_methods(['POST'])
def hx_get_slot_details(request: OtovoRequest) -> HttpResponse:
    form = SlotDetailsForm(data=request.POST)
    if not form.is_valid():
        return HttpResponseBadRequest(form.errors)

    travel_to_slot = form.cleaned_data['start_time'] - form.cleaned_data['travel_to_slot_start_time']
    travel_from_slot = form.cleaned_data['travel_from_slot_end_time'] - form.cleaned_data['end_time']
    combined_travel = travel_from_slot + travel_to_slot

    destination_address_id = form.cleaned_data['destination_address_id']
    previous_address_id = form.cleaned_data['previous_address_id']
    next_address_id = form.cleaned_data['next_address_id']
    formatted_addresses = dict(
        Address.objects.filter(
            id__in=[
                destination_address_id,
                previous_address_id,
                next_address_id,
            ]
        ).values_list('id', 'formatted_address')
    )

    schedule: FieldSalesSchedule = form.cleaned_data['schedule']

    # Recommended travel time limit
    LIMIT = timedelta(minutes=90)

    context: dict[str, Any] = {
        'score': f'{form.cleaned_data["score"]:.4f}',
        'destination_address': formatted_addresses[destination_address_id],
        'previous_address': formatted_addresses[previous_address_id],
        'next_address': formatted_addresses[next_address_id],
        'previous_is_base': schedule.base_address.id == previous_address_id,
        'next_is_base': schedule.base_address.id == next_address_id,
        'schedule_summary': schedule.agent.get_schedule_summary(),
        'form': form,
        'slot_display': AgentCalendarEvent.format_slot(
            form.cleaned_data['start_time'], form.cleaned_data['end_time']
        ),
        'travel_times': {
            'to_slot': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(travel_to_slot.total_seconds()),
            'from_slot': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(travel_from_slot.total_seconds()),
            'total': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(combined_travel.total_seconds()),
            'total_warning': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(combined_travel.total_seconds()),
            'to_slot_warning': travel_to_slot.total_seconds() > LIMIT.total_seconds(),
            'from_slot_warning': travel_from_slot.total_seconds() > LIMIT.total_seconds(),
        },
        'combined_travel': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(combined_travel.total_seconds()),
        'waypoints': [],
        'route_data': {},
    }

    # Addresses in the order they will be visited
    ordered_address_ids = [previous_address_id, destination_address_id, next_address_id]
    addresses: list[Address] = list(
        Address.objects.filter(id__in=ordered_address_ids)
        .annotate(
            travel_order=Case(
                *[When(id=id, then=pos) for pos, id in enumerate(ordered_address_ids)],
                output_field=IntegerField(),
            )
        )
        .order_by('travel_order')
    )

    if next_address_id == previous_address_id:
        # If we're driving back-to-back to the base address, it will be excluded
        # in the QS filter above, so we add it back in.
        addresses.append(addresses[0])

    # Safeguard for icon and type lookup below
    assert len(addresses) <= 3, 'Only 3 addresses are supported'
    route_response = fetch_route(addresses)
    if route_response:
        context |= {
            'total_distance_km': route_response.meters / 1000,
            'waypoints': [
                {
                    'type': ['Start', 'Home Visit', 'Next'][i],
                    'icon': [
                        'fa-regular fa-car',
                        'fa-regular fa-location-dot',
                        'fa-regular fa-right-to-line',
                    ][i],
                    'address': formatted_addresses[addr.id],
                    'coords': [addr.point.y, addr.point.x],  # Leaflet uses [lat, lng]
                }
                for i, addr in enumerate(addresses)
                if addr.point is not None
            ],
            'route_data': {
                'coordinates': [[p[1], p[0]] for p in route_response.route.coords],
                'bbox': route_response.bbox if route_response else None,
            },
        }

    return TemplateResponse(request, template='interests/slot_details_partial.html', context=context)
```

Yes, we could make this cleaner by reconstructing the SlotWithTravelTime/SlotWithScore object from the form data. Here's a suggested refactor:

```python
@dataclass
class SlotWithTravelTime:
    # ... existing fields ...

    # Add class-level constant
    RECOMMENDED_TRAVEL_LIMIT = timedelta(minutes=90)

    @classmethod
    def from_form_data(cls, form: SlotDetailsForm) -> "SlotWithTravelTime":
        """Create a slot object from form data"""
        start_time = form.cleaned_data['start_time']
        end_time = form.cleaned_data['end_time']
        travel_to_start = form.cleaned_data['travel_to_slot_start_time']
        travel_from_end = form.cleaned_data['travel_from_slot_end_time']

        return cls(
            slot=DateTimeTZRange(start_time, end_time),
            travel_time_to_destination=DateTimeTZRange(
                travel_to_start,
                start_time
            ),
            travel_time_from_destination=DateTimeTZRange(
                end_time,
                travel_from_end
            ),
            destination=form.cleaned_data['destination_address_id'],
            previous_address=form.cleaned_data['previous_address_id'],
            next_address=form.cleaned_data['next_address_id'],
            agent_id=form.cleaned_data['schedule'].agent.id,
            schedule_id=form.cleaned_data['schedule'].id,
        )

    @property
    def travel_times(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
        """Get formatted travel times and warnings"""
        to_slot = self.travel_duration_to_destination
        from_slot = self.travel_duration_from_destination
        combined = self.combined_travel_duration

        return {
            'to_slot': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(to_slot),
            'from_slot': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(from_slot),
            'total': AgentCalendarEvent.format_duration(combined),
            'to_slot_warning': timedelta(seconds=to_slot) > self.RECOMMENDED_TRAVEL_LIMIT,
            'from_slot_warning': timedelta(seconds=from_slot) > self.RECOMMENDED_TRAVEL_LIMIT,
        }

    def get_waypoint_data(self, formatted_addresses: dict[UUID, str]) -> list[dict]:
        """Get waypoint data for map rendering"""
        addresses = [self.previous_address, self.destination, self.next_address]
        
        return [
            {
                'type': ['Start', 'Home Visit', 'Next'][i],
                'icon': [
                    'fa-regular fa-car',
                    'fa-regular fa-location-dot',
                    'fa-regular fa-right-to-line',
                ][i],
                'address': formatted_addresses[addr.id],
                'coords': [addr.point.y, addr.point.x],
            }
            for i, addr in enumerate(addresses)
            if addr.point is not None
        ]

    @property
    def ordered_addresses(self) -> list[Address]:
        """Get addresses in travel order"""
        addresses = [self.previous_address, self.destination, self.next_address]
        if self.next_address.id == self.previous_address.id:
            addresses.append(self.previous_address)
        return addresses

@dataclass
class SlotWithScore(SlotWithTravelTime):
    # ... existing fields ...

    @classmethod
    def from_form_data(cls, form: SlotDetailsForm) -> "SlotWithScore":
        """Create a scored slot from form data"""
        slot = super().from_form_data(form)
        return cls(
            **asdict(slot),
            score=form.cleaned_data['score'],
            rank=form.cleaned_data['rank']
        )
```

```ts cloud/cloud/rainbow/modules/rainbow/js/slide-over.ts
import onLoad from '../../../../static/js/onLoad';

function hideAll() {
  const toHideSlideOvers = document.querySelectorAll(
    '._slide-over-panel:not(._translate-x-full)'
  );
  for (let i = 0; i < toHideSlideOvers.length; ++i) {
    const parent = toHideSlideOvers[i].closest('[role="dialog"]');
    toHideSlideOvers[i].classList.remove('_translate-x-0', '_drop-shadow-md');
    toHideSlideOvers[i].classList.add('_translate-x-full');

    setTimeout(() => {
      // To ensure the exit-transition finishes before hiding the menu,
      // this need to be coordinated with the `_duration-300` class
      // on the SlideOver() macro.
      parent.classList.add('_hidden');
    }, 300);
  }

  // For detecting from other places when the slide-over is closed
  document.dispatchEvent(new Event('slideOverClosed'));
}

function correctImageAspectRatio(images) {
  // This is a function to add padding to vertical images so that they don't
  // take up the entire slide-over.

  if (!images) {
    return;
  }

  for (let i = 0; i < images.length; i++) {
    const image = images[i];
    if (
      image.classList.contains('_object-cover') &&
      image.width / image.height < 1
    ) {
      // Add padding to the wrapping div
      image.closest('div').classList.add('_px-20');
    }
  }
}
let currentContainer: HTMLDivElement | null = null;

function handleClickOutside(e) {
  const toastContainer = document.querySelector('#toasts-container');
  if (toastContainer && toastContainer.contains(e.target)) {
    return;
  }

  if (currentContainer && !currentContainer.contains(e.target)) {
    hideAll();
  }
}

function handleKeydown(keyPressEvent) {
  // Let users press escape to remove the dialog
  const ESC_KEY = 27;

  if (currentContainer && keyPressEvent.keyCode === ESC_KEY) {
    if (currentContainer.querySelector('sl-dropdown[open]')) {
      // Ignore ESC press if the slide-over have an open dropdown
      return;
    }
    hideAll();
  }
}

function show(event: Event) {
  event.stopPropagation();

  // This ID need to match the derived SlideOver() ID in
  // cloud/rainbow/jinja2/rainbow/components/slideOver/macros.html

  const slideOverContainer = document.getElementById(`${this.id}-container`);
  currentContainer = slideOverContainer;
  const slideOverParent = slideOverContainer.closest('[role="dialog"]');
  const isAlreadyVisible =
    slideOverContainer.classList.contains('_translate-x-0');

  const images = slideOverContainer.querySelectorAll('img');
  correctImageAspectRatio(images);

  if (slideOverContainer) {
    if (!isAlreadyVisible) {
      hideAll();

      window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
        // First we remove the hidden class...
        slideOverParent.classList.remove('_hidden');
        window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
          // Then we translate it out.
          slideOverContainer.classList.remove('_translate-x-full');
          slideOverContainer.classList.add('_translate-x-0', '_drop-shadow-md');
        });
      });
    } else {
      // Clicking the trigger after it's open, should close it again.
      hideAll();
    }
  }
}

function hide() {
  hideAll();
}

function createEventsForFragment(frag) {
  const triggerShowElements = frag.querySelectorAll(
    '._slide-over-show-trigger'
  );
  for (let i = 0; i < triggerShowElements.length; ++i) {
    triggerShowElements[i].addEventListener('click', show);
  }

  const triggerHideElements = frag.querySelectorAll(
    '._slide-over-hide-trigger'
  );
  for (let i = 0; i < triggerHideElements.length; ++i) {
    triggerHideElements[i].addEventListener('click', hide);
  }
}

function createEvents() {
  createEventsForFragment(document);
  window.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeydown);
  window.addEventListener('click', handleClickOutside);
  window.addEventListener('hideAllSlideOvers', hideAll);

}

onLoad(() => {
  createEvents();
  if (typeof htmx !== 'undefined') {
    htmx.onLoad((target) => {
      // Add events for any slideover elemnts in the htmx fragment
      createEventsForFragment(target);
    });
  }
});
```

I want to add a feature that will automatically open a slide over on load if the URL contains a specific hash. I will add a new function to the `slideOver.js` file that will check the URL for a hash and open the corresponding slide over if it exists. I will also add a new event listener to the `createEvents` function that will call this new function when the page loads.


Here's how to add that feature to the slide-over.ts file:

```typescript
// Add function to check and open slide-over based on hash
function handleHashChange() {
  const hash = window.location.hash;
  if (!hash) return;

  // Remove the # from the hash
  const slideOverId = hash.substring(1);
  const trigger = document.querySelector(`[id="${slideOverId}"]`);
  
  if (trigger && trigger.classList.contains('_slide-over-show-trigger')) {
    // Use existing show function with a synthetic event
    show.call(trigger, new Event('hashchange'));
  }
}

// Update createEvents to include hash handling
function createEvents() {
  createEventsForFragment(document);
  window.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeydown);
  window.addEventListener('click', handleClickOutside);
  window.addEventListener('hideAllSlideOvers', hideAll);
  
  // Add hash change listener
  window.addEventListener('hashchange', handleHashChange);
}

// Update the show function to handle URL
function show(event: Event) {
  event.stopPropagation();

  const slideOverContainer = document.getElementById(`${this.id}-container`);
  currentContainer = slideOverContainer;
  const slideOverParent = slideOverContainer?.closest('[role="dialog"]');
  const isAlreadyVisible = slideOverContainer?.classList.contains('_translate-x-0');

  if (slideOverContainer && slideOverParent) {
    const images = slideOverContainer.querySelectorAll('img');
    correctImageAspectRatio(images);

    if (!isAlreadyVisible) {
      hideAll();

      // Update URL hash unless this was triggered by a hashchange
      if (event.type !== 'hashchange') {
        window.history.pushState(null, '', `#${this.id}`);
      }

      window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
        slideOverParent.classList.remove('_hidden');
        window.requestAnimationFrame(() => {
          slideOverContainer.classList.remove('_translate-x-full');
          slideOverContainer.classList.add('_translate-x-0', '_drop-shadow-md');
        });
      });
    } else {
      hideAll();
    }
  }
}

// Update hide to clear hash
function hide() {
  // Remove hash when closing if it matches current slide-over
  if (window.location.hash && currentContainer) {
    const currentId = currentContainer.id.replace('-container', '');
    if (window.location.hash === `#${currentId}`) {
      window.history.pushState(null, '', window.location.pathname);
    }
  }
  hideAll();
}

// Update onLoad to check hash on initial page load
onLoad(() => {
  createEvents();
  if (typeof htmx !== 'undefined') {
    htmx.onLoad((target) => {
      createEventsForFragment(target);
    });
  }
  // Check for hash on initial load
  handleHashChange();
});
```

what's the benefit of doing show.call instead of calling show with arguments?
`show.call(trigger, event)` and `show(event)` are different because of how `this` works in JavaScript/TypeScript.

In the original `show` function, `this` is used to access the trigger element's ID:
```typescript
function show(event: Event) {
  // ...
  const slideOverContainer = document.getElementById(`${this.id}-container`);
  // ...
}
```

If we just called `show(event)`, `this` would be either:
- `undefined` in strict mode
- The global object (window) in non-strict mode

By using `show.call(trigger, event)`, we explicitly set what `this` refers to inside the function. It's equivalent to temporarily binding the function to that element.

Here are the different ways we could handle this, from worst to best:

```typescript
// BAD - this would be undefined/window
show(event);

// WORKS but less clear - passes trigger as parameter
function show(event: Event, triggerElement: HTMLElement) {
  const slideOverContainer = document.getElementById(`${triggerElement.id}-container`);
}
show(event, trigger);

// BETTER - explicitly shows we're setting function context
show.call(trigger, event);

// ALSO GOOD - using bind to create a bound function
const boundShow = show.bind(trigger);
boundShow(event);
```

The `.call()` approach is commonly used when you want to:
1. Preserve the original function's design/interface
2. Be explicit about the execution context
3. Temporarily bind a function without creating a new function

In this case, it keeps the code consistent with how the function is used in the click event listeners while allowing us to programmatically trigger it with the correct `this` context.
but how do I type this?
Ah, good point! Here's how to properly type `this` in TypeScript:

```typescript
// Define the type of elements that can trigger the slide-over
type SlideOverTrigger = HTMLElement & {
  id: string;  // Ensure id exists
};

function show(this: SlideOverTrigger, event: Event) {
  event.stopPropagation();

  const slideOverContainer = document.getElementById(`${this.id}-container`);
  // ... rest of function
}

function handleNavigation() {
  const hash = window.location.hash;
  if (!hash) {
    if (currentContainer) {
      hideAll();
    }
    return;
  }

  const slideOverId = hash.substring(1);
  const trigger = document.querySelector(`[id="${slideOverId}"]`);
  
  if (trigger && trigger.classList.contains('_slide-over-show-trigger')) {
    // Now TypeScript knows 'this' must be SlideOverTrigger
    show.call(trigger as SlideOverTrigger, new Event('navigation'));
  }
}
```

The key is using the `this` parameter annotation in the function declaration. This tells TypeScript what type `this` will be inside the function, and it will enforce that type when the function is called with `.call()`, `.apply()`, or `.bind()`.
